subject: Mathematics
q: All the letters of the word PUBLIC are written in all possible orders and these words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. Then the serial number of the word PUBLIC is
question: All the letters of the word PUBLIC are written in all possible orders and these words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. Then the serial number of the word PUBLIC is
solution:
The given word is PUBLIC

Arranging the letters alphabetically, we get

BCILPU

When the word starts with any of the letters B/C/I/L, the number of possibilities=5!×4=480

Now when the word starts with PB, then the number of possibilities=4!=24

Now when the word starts with PC, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with PI, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with PL, then the number of possibilities =4!=24

Now when the word starts with PUBC, then the number of possibilities=2!=2

Now when the word starts with PUBI, then the number of possibilities =2!=2

Now when the word starts with PUBLC, then the number of possibilities=1

Now when the word starts with PUBLIC, then the number of possibilities =1

Rank =480+24×4+2×2+1×2=582

Hence, rank of the work PUBLIC is 582.

q: There rotten apples are mixed accidently with seven good apples and four apples are drawn one by one without replacement. Let the random variable X denote the number of rotten apples. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> represent mean and variance of X, respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>μ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: There rotten apples are mixed accidently with seven good apples and four apples are drawn one by one without replacement. Let the random variable X denote the number of rotten apples. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> represent mean and variance of X, respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>μ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
There rotten apples are mixed accidently with seven good apples,

And four apples are drawn one by one without replacement.

Now the random variable X denote the number of rotten apples.

And μ and σ2 represent mean and variance of X, respectively, 

So, now plotting the table of given data we get,

x P(x) xP(X) x2P(X)
0 16 0 0
1 12 12 12
2 310 610 1210
3 130 110 930

Now mean is given by, ∑xP(x)=62=μ

And variance is given by σ2=∑x2P(x)-μ2

So, by putting the value from above table we get,  σ2+μ2=0+12+1210+930=2

Hence, 10σ2+μ2=20

question: If vertex of parabola is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and equation of its directrix is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>21</mn></math>, then the length of latus rectum is
solution:
Plotting the graph with given data we have,

We know that, in any parabola, the length of the latus rectum =4a and perpendicular distance from vertex to the directrix =a

So, a=8+3-2142+-32

⇒a=-105=2 

Therefore, the length of the latus rectum 4a=4×2=8

from diagram

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>}</mo></math>. Then the number of possible functions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>A</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>·</mo><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>m</mi></mfenced><mo>·</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>n</mi></mfenced></math> for every <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>A</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>·</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>}</mo></math>. Then the number of possible functions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>A</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>·</mo><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>m</mi></mfenced><mo>·</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>n</mi></mfenced></math> for every <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>A</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>·</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

fm·n=fm·fn

Taking m=1

⇒f1·n=f1·fn

⇒f1=1

Now taking m=3 & n=3 we get,

f(9)=f(3)×f(3),

So, f3, f9 have two possibility 1,1 & 3,9

Now taking m=2 & n=1 we get,

f2·1=f2·f1

Now here f1=1, so f2 can take all 6 numbers,

Similarly, for f5 & f8 there will be 6 ways each,

So, total possible function will be, =1×6×2×6×6×1=432

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of solutions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>7</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>7</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>[</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>]</mo></math>  is equal to
question: The number of solutions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>7</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>7</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>[</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>]</mo></math>  is equal to
solution:
We know that 0≤sin2x≤1 and 0≤cos2x≤1

Also, we know for a number which lie between 0 to 1, higher the power of the number the less is its value.  

⇒sin7x≤sin2x≤1   …1

and cos7x≤cos2x≤1   …2

Also, we know that sin2x+cos2x=1

This means the equality must hold for 1 and 2

⇒sin7x=sin2x and cos7x=cos2x

⇒sin2xsin5x-1=0 and cos2xcos5x-1=0

⇒sinx=0 or sinx=1 and cosx=0 or cosx=1⇒x=0, 2π, 4π, π2, 5π2.

Thus, there are total 5 solutions.

subject: Mathematics
q: $z$ and $w$ are two non zero complex no.s such that $|z|=|w|$ and $\operatorname{Arg} z+\operatorname{Arg} w=\pi$ then $z$ equals
question: $z$ and $w$ are two non zero complex no.s such that $|z|=|w|$ and $\operatorname{Arg} z+\operatorname{Arg} w=\pi$ then $z$ equals
solution: Let $|\mathrm{z}|=|\omega|=\mathrm{r} \quad \therefore \mathrm{z}=\mathrm{re}^{\mathrm{i} \theta}, \omega=\mathrm{re}^{\mathrm{i} \phi}$ where $\theta+\phi=\pi \quad \therefore \bar{\omega}=\mathrm{re}^{-\mathrm{i \phi}}$ $\therefore z=\mathrm{re}^{i(\pi-\phi)}=\mathrm{re}^{\mathrm{i} \pi} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-i \phi}=-\mathrm{re}^{-i \phi}=-\bar{\omega}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let [.] denote the greatest integer function. If $\int_0^{e^3}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}-1}}\right] \mathrm{dx}=\alpha-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2$, then $\alpha^3$ is equal to _______ .
question: Let [.] denote the greatest integer function. If $\int_0^{e^3}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}-1}}\right] \mathrm{dx}=\alpha-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2$, then $\alpha^3$ is equal to _______ .
solution:
$f(x)=\frac{1}{e^{x-1}}=e^{1-x}$
\begin{array}{c|c}\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=1 \\\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}-1}}=2 & \mathrm{x}=1 \\\mathrm{x}=1-\ln 2 &\end{array}
$f(0)=e^1=2.71$
$f\left(e^3\right)=e^{1-e^3} \in(0,1)$
$\mathrm{I}=\int_0^{1-\ell \mathrm{n} 2} 2 \mathrm{dx}+\int_{1-\ell \mathrm{n} 2}^1 1 \mathrm{dx}+\int_1^{\mathrm{e}^3} 0 \mathrm{dx}$
$=2(1-\ell \operatorname{n} 2-0)+1(1-1+\ln 2)+0$
$\alpha-\ln 2=2-\ln 2$
$\alpha=2$
$\alpha^3=8$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the mean of the data
Class$5-10$$10-15$$15-20$$20-25$$25-30$$30-35$
Frequency$2$$k$$28$$54$$k+1$$5$

is $21$, then $k$ is one of the roots of the equation :
question:
If the mean of the data
Class$5-10$$10-15$$15-20$$20-25$$25-30$$30-35$
Frequency$2$$k$$28$$54$$k+1$$5$

is $21$, then $k$ is one of the roots of the equation :
solution:
The class marks (mid-points) $x_i$ for the given classes are $7.5, 12.5, 17.5, 22.5, 27.5, 32.5$.

The sum of the frequencies is:
$\sum f_i = 2 + k + 28 + 54 + (k + 1) + 5 = 2k + 90$

The sum of the products $f_i x_i$ is:
$\sum f_i x_i = 2(7.5) + k(12.5) + 28(17.5) + 54(22.5) + (k + 1)(27.5) + 5(32.5)$

$\sum f_i x_i = 15 + 12.5k + 490 + 1215 + 27.5k + 27.5 + 162.5$

$\sum f_i x_i = 40k + 1910$

The mean is given as $21$. Using the formula for the mean $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$:

$\dfrac{40k + 1910}{2k + 90} = 21$

$40k + 1910 = 21(2k + 90)$

$40k + 1910 = 42k + 1890$

$2k = 20 \Rightarrow k = 10$

Now, substituting $x = 10$ into the given options to check which equation has $10$ as a root:

For $2x^2 - 19x - 10 = 0$:
$2(10)^2 - 19(10) - 10 = 200 - 190 - 10 = 0$

Thus, $k = 10$ is a root of $2x^2 - 19x - 10 = 0$.

Answer: $2x^2 - 19x - 10 = 0$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $p$ be the statement " $x$ is an irrational number", $q$ be the statement " $y$ is a transcendental number", and $r$ be the statement " $\mathrm{x}$ is a rational number iff $\mathrm{y}$ is a transcendental number".
Statement $-1$ : $r$ is equivalent to either $q$ or $p$
Statement $-2: r$ is equivalent to $\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)$.
question:
Let $p$ be the statement " $x$ is an irrational number", $q$ be the statement " $y$ is a transcendental number", and $r$ be the statement " $\mathrm{x}$ is a rational number iff $\mathrm{y}$ is a transcendental number".
Statement $-1$ : $r$ is equivalent to either $q$ or $p$
Statement $-2: r$ is equivalent to $\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)$.
options: Statement $-1$ is false, Statement $-2$ is true
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true, Statement $-2$ is a correct explanation for Statement $-1$
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is not a correct explanation for Statement $-1$.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is false.
solution:
Given statement $r=\sim p \leftrightarrow q$
Statement $-1: r_1=(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$
Statement $-2: r_2=\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)=(p \wedge q) \vee(\sim q \wedge \sim p)$
From the truth table of $r, r_1$ and $r_2$, $r=r_1$.
Hence Statement $-1$ is true and Statement $-2$ is false.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that $2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\vec{c} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}$ is equal to
question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that $2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\vec{c} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}$ is equal to
solution:
$2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c}) + 3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c}) = \vec{0} \Rightarrow (2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}) \times \vec{c} = \vec{0}$.
So $\vec{c} \parallel (2\vec{a} + 3\vec{b}) = (7, -13, 19)$. Let $\vec{c} = t(7, -13, 19)$.
$\vec{a} - \vec{b} = (1, -4, 2)$.
$(\vec{a} - \vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c} = t(7 + 52 + 38) = 97t = -97 \Rightarrow t = -1$.
$\vec{c} = (-7, 13, -19)$.
$\vec{c} \times \hat{k} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -7 & 13 & -19 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (13, 7, 0)$.
$|\vec{c} \times \hat{k}|^2 = 169 + 49 = 218$.

q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, cos2π7+cos4π7+cos6π7

=12sinπ72cos2π7sinπ7+2cos4π7sinπ7+2cos6π7sinπ7

=12sinπ7sin3π7-sinπ7+sin5π7-sin3π7+sin7π7-sin5π7

Since, 2cosAsinB=sinA+B-sinA-B

=12sinπ7-sinπ7+sinπ

=-12, ∵sinπ=0

q: The sum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>21</mn></munderover><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to
question: The sum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>21</mn></munderover><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to
solution:
∑n=12134n-14n+3=34∑n=12114n-1-14n+3

=3413-17+17-111+….+183-187

=3413-187=3484261=729

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $X=\mathbf{R} \times \mathbf{R}$. Define a relation $R$ on $X$ as :
$\left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_2, b_2\right) \Leftrightarrow b_1=b_2$

Statement I : $\quad \mathrm{R}$ is an equivalence relation.
Statement II : For some $(a, b) \in X$, the set $S=\{(x, y) \in X:(x, y) R(a, b)\}$ represents a line parallel to $y=x$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Let $X=\mathbf{R} \times \mathbf{R}$. Define a relation $R$ on $X$ as :
$\left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_2, b_2\right) \Leftrightarrow b_1=b_2$

Statement I : $\quad \mathrm{R}$ is an equivalence relation.
Statement II : For some $(a, b) \in X$, the set $S=\{(x, y) \in X:(x, y) R(a, b)\}$ represents a line parallel to $y=x$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Reflexive : $\left(a_1, b\right) R\left(a_1, b_1\right) \Rightarrow b_1=b_1 \quad$ True

$\left.\begin{array}{rl}\text { Symmetric : } & \left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_2, b_2\right) \Rightarrow b_1=b_2 \\ & \left(a_2, b_2\right) R\left(a_1, b_1\right) \Rightarrow b_2=b_1\end{array}\right\}$ True

$\begin{aligned} \text { Transitive : } & \left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_2, b_2\right) \Rightarrow b_1=b_2 \\ & \&\left(a_2, b_2\right) R\left(a_3, b_3\right) b_2=b_3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(a_1, b_1\right) R\left(a_3 . b_3\right) \Rightarrow \text { True }\} b_1=b_3\end{aligned}$

Hence Relation $R$ is an equivence relation Statement-I is true.
For statement $-\mathrm{II} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{b}$ so False

subject: Mathematics
q: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mi>s</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mi>s</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
Negation of given statement is

=~~s∨~r∧s

Using De Morgan's law ~A∨B=~A∧~B

=~~s∧~~ r ∧s

Using double negation law ~~A=A, and De Morgan's law

=s∧~~r∨~s

=s∧r∨~s 

Using distributive law A∧B∨C=A∧B∨A∧C

=s∧r∨s∧~s

=s∧r∨F

=s∧r.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f(x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} x$ and $g(x)=\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}$. Then the domain of $f \circ g$ is
question: Let $f(x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} x$ and $g(x)=\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}$. Then the domain of $f \circ g$ is
solution:
$f(g(x))=\ln \left(\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}\right)$

Since $2 x^2-2 x+1>0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \because(-2)^2-4(2) < 0$
Consider
$\begin{aligned}
& x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2 \\
& =\left(x^4-2 x^3+x^2\right)+\left(x^2-2 x+1\right)+\left(1+x^2\right) \\
& =x^2(x-1)^2+(x-1)^2+\left(x^2+1\right)>0 \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\
& \Rightarrow g(x)>0 \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\
& \Rightarrow \ln f((x)), f(x)>0 \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \\
& \Rightarrow x \in \mathbb{R} \text { is domain }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $100$ times the $100^{\text {th }}$ term of an $AP$ with non zero common difference equals the $50$ times its $50^{\text {th }}$ term, then the $150^{\text {th }}$ term of this $A P$ is
question: If $100$ times the $100^{\text {th }}$ term of an $AP$ with non zero common difference equals the $50$ times its $50^{\text {th }}$ term, then the $150^{\text {th }}$ term of this $A P$ is
solution: $100\left(T_{100}\right)=50\left(T_{50}\right) \quad \Rightarrow 2[a+99 d]=a+49 d \Rightarrow a+149 d=0 \quad \Rightarrow T_{150}=0$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the tangent to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is also a tangent to the ellipse, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> then <math><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to:
question: If the tangent to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is also a tangent to the ellipse, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> then <math><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Since α, β lies on the parabola ⇒α=β2

⇒α, β=β2, β

Tangent to a parabola y2=4ax at a point x1, y1 is yy1=2ax+x1

Therefore, tangent to the parabola y2=x at β2, β isy⋅β=12x+β2

⇒y=x2β+β2   ...i

Tangent to parabola is also a tangent to ellipse

x2+2y2=1 ⇒x2+y212=1

And, a line y=mx+c is a tangent to the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 if c2=a2m2+b2

Using the condition c2=a2m2+b2, we get

β24=1212β2+12

⇒β24=14β2+12

⇒β4-2β2-1=0

Using Sridharacharya's Formula the roots of a quadratic equation ax2+bx+c=0, a≠0 are x=-b±b2-4ac2a

⇒β2=--2±-22-4×1×-12×1

⇒β2=2±82

⇒β2=1+2 or β2=1-2 (Neglected as β2 is positive)

⇒β2=2+1

⇒α=2+1.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the number of words, with or without meaning. which can be made using all the letters of the word MATHEMATICS in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> do not come together, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo><mo>)</mo><mi>k</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
question: If the number of words, with or without meaning. which can be made using all the letters of the word MATHEMATICS in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> do not come together, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>!</mo><mo>)</mo><mi>k</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
The word 'MATHEMATICS' consists of 11 letters including two M's, two T's and two A's,

Now, first we will arrange letters _M_A_T_H_E_M_A_T_I_.

Here, we have 10 gaps to fill C & S.

The number of words with or without meaning can be formed from the word MATHEMATICS when C, S does not come together is

=9!2!·2!·2!×C210×2!

=9×8×7×6!2!·2!×10×92

=5670

Hence this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math><msup><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sec</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mrow></math> and <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> , then <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: If <math><msup><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sec</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mrow></math> and <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> , then <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
f'x=tan-1⁡sec⁡x+tan⁡x=tan-1⁡1+sin⁡xcos⁡x=tan-1⁡1-cos⁡π2+xsin⁡π2+x

⇒f'(x)=tan-1⁡2sin2⁡π4+x22sin⁡π4+x2cos⁡π4+x2

⇒f'xdx=π4+x2dx

⇒fx=π4x+x24+c

∵f0=0⇒c=0

So, f1=π+14

subject: Mathematics
q: A building has ground floor and 10 more floors. Nine persons enter in a lift at the ground floor. The lift goes up to the $10^{\text{th}}$ floor. The number of ways, in which any 4 persons exit at a floor and the remaining 5 persons exit at a different floor, if the lift does not stop at the first and the second floors, is equal to :
question: A building has ground floor and 10 more floors. Nine persons enter in a lift at the ground floor. The lift goes up to the $10^{\text{th}}$ floor. The number of ways, in which any 4 persons exit at a floor and the remaining 5 persons exit at a different floor, if the lift does not stop at the first and the second floors, is equal to :
solution:
Total number of persons = $9$

The lift goes up to the $10^{\text{th}}$ floor but does not stop at the $1^{\text{st}}$ and $2^{\text{nd}}$ floors. Thus, the available floors for the persons to exit are from the $3^{\text{rd}}$ to the $10^{\text{th}}$ floor.

Number of available floors = $8$

The number of ways to select $4$ persons out of $9$ to form the first group is $^{9}C_{4}$. The remaining $5$ persons automatically form the second group.

$^{9}C_{4} = \dfrac{9 \times 8 \times 7 \times 6}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1} = 126$

Now, these two distinct groups must exit at two different floors out of the $8$ available floors. The number of ways to assign $2$ different floors to the $2$ groups is $^{8}P_{2}$.

$^{8}P_{2} = 8 \times 7 = 56$

Total number of ways = $126 \times 56 = 7056$

Answer: $7056$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Given three indentical bags each containing 10 balls, whose colours are as follows :
$\begin{array}{cccc} & \text{Red} & \text{Blue} & \text{Green} \\ \text{Bag I} & 3 & 2 & 5 \\ \text{Bag II} & 4 & 3 & 3 \\ \text{Bag III} & 5 & 1 & 4\end{array}$
A person chooses a bag at random and takes out a ball. If the ball is Red, the probability that it is from bag I is p and if the balls is Green, the probability that it is from bag III is q , then the value of $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{p}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{q}}\right)$ is :
question:
Given three indentical bags each containing 10 balls, whose colours are as follows :
$\begin{array}{cccc} & \text{Red} & \text{Blue} & \text{Green} \\ \text{Bag I} & 3 & 2 & 5 \\ \text{Bag II} & 4 & 3 & 3 \\ \text{Bag III} & 5 & 1 & 4\end{array}$
A person chooses a bag at random and takes out a ball. If the ball is Red, the probability that it is from bag I is p and if the balls is Green, the probability that it is from bag III is q , then the value of $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{p}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{q}}\right)$ is :
solution:
$\mathrm{p}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{I}}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{3}{10}\right)}{\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{3}{10}+\frac{4}{10}+\frac{5}{10}\right)}=\frac{1}{4}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{q}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{III}}}{\mathrm{G}}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{4}{10}\right)}{\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{5}{10}+\frac{3}{10}+\frac{4}{10}\right)}=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{\mathrm{p}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{q}}=7\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the mirror image of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> with respect to the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>19</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mi>β</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>γ</mi></math> is equal to  ______.
question: Let the mirror image of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> with respect to the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>19</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mi>β</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>γ</mi></math> is equal to  ______.
solution:
Given the mirror image of Pa,b,c is P'a-6,β,γ

The mid-point M=a-3,β+b2,γ+c2

Since M lies on 3x-4y+12z+19=0

⇒3a-9-4β+b2+12γ+c2+19=0

⇒6a-18-4β-4b+12γ+12c+38=0

⇒6a-4b+12c-4β+12γ+20=0      ⋯1

Since PP' is parallel to normal of the plane then

63=b-β-4=c-γ12

⇒b=β-8, c=γ+24

Given a+b+c=5⇒a+β-8+γ+24=5

⇒a=-β-γ-11

Now putting these values in 1 we get

6-β-γ-11-4β-8+12γ+24-4β+12γ+20=0

⇒7β-9γ=170-33=137

q: If the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>AB</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>AC</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are the sides of a triangle <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo> </mrow> </math> then the length of the median through <math> <mi>A</mi> </math> is:
question: If the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>AB</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>AC</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are the sides of a triangle <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo> </mrow> </math> then the length of the median through <math> <mi>A</mi> </math> is:
solution:
Length of median =AB→+AC→2

=8i^-2j^+8k^2

= 3 3

subject: Mathematics
q:
For the four circles M, N, O and P, following four equations are given:

Circle M : x2+y2=1

Circle N : x2+y2-2x=0

Circle O : x2+y2-2x-2y+1=0

Circle P : x2+y2-2y=0

If the centre of circle M is joined with centre of the circle N, further centre of circle N is joined with centre of the circle O, centre of circle O is joined with the centre of circle P and lastly, centre of circle P is joined with centre of circle M, then these lines form the sides of a
question:
For the four circles M, N, O and P, following four equations are given:

Circle M : x2+y2=1

Circle N : x2+y2-2x=0

Circle O : x2+y2-2x-2y+1=0

Circle P : x2+y2-2y=0

If the centre of circle M is joined with centre of the circle N, further centre of circle N is joined with centre of the circle O, centre of circle O is joined with the centre of circle P and lastly, centre of circle P is joined with centre of circle M, then these lines form the sides of a
solution:
M : x2+y2=1 center=0, 0

N : x2+y2-2x=0, center=1, 0

O : x2+y2-2x-2y+1=0, center=1, 1

P : x2+y2-2y=0, center=0, 1

MN=1-02+0-02=1

NO=1-12+0-12=1

OP=1-02+1-12=1

PM=0-12+1-12=1

Then, all the sides are equal of a quadrilateral then MNOP is a square.

subject: Mathematics
q: The points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>82</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>30</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>82</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>30</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The points, A0,83,B1,3,C82,30 are collinear, as slope of AB=slope of BC, i.e.,

3-831-0= 30-382-1=13

Hence, option (Lie on a straight line) is correct.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the number of 5-element subsets of the set $A=\left\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{20}\right\}$ of 20 distinct elements is $k$ times the number of 5-element subsets containing $a_4$, then $k$ is
question: If the number of 5-element subsets of the set $A=\left\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{20}\right\}$ of 20 distinct elements is $k$ times the number of 5-element subsets containing $a_4$, then $k$ is
solution:
Set $A=\left\{a_1, a_2, \ldots . ., a_{20}\right\}$ has 20 distinct elements.
We have to select 5-element subset.
$\therefore$ Number of 5-element subsets $={ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_5$
According to question
$$
\begin{aligned}
& { }^{20} \mathrm{C}_5=\left({ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_4 \cdot k\right. \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{20 !}{5 ! 15 !}=k \cdot\left(\frac{19 !}{4 ! 15 !}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{20}{5}=k \Rightarrow k=4
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations $2 x+2 y+3 z=a$

$3 x-y+5 z=b$

$x-3 y+2 z=c$

where, $a, b,$ care non-zero real numbers, has more than onc solution, then
question:
If the system of linear equations $2 x+2 y+3 z=a$

$3 x-y+5 z=b$

$x-3 y+2 z=c$

where, $a, b,$ care non-zero real numbers, has more than onc solution, then
solution:
$\because$ System of equations has more than one solution

$\therefore \Delta=\Delta_{1}=\Delta_{2}=\Delta_{3}=0$ for infinite solution

$\begin{aligned}

\Delta_{1} &=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

a & 2 & 3 \\

b & -1 & 5 \\

c & -3 & 2

\end{array}\right|=a(13)+2(5 c-2 b)+3(-3 b+c) \\

&=13 a-13 b+13 c=0

\end{aligned}$

i.c, $a-b+c=0$

or $\quad b-c-a=0$
